structure.affirmative: Subject + will + V1 — I will call. / She will help.
structure.negative: Subject + won't + V1 — I won't call. / She won't come.
structure.question: Will + Subject + V1? — Will you call? / Will she come?

usage.text: Spontaneous decisions made at the moment

faq.q: What's the difference between 'will' and 'going to'?
faq.a: 'Will' = spontaneous decision or prediction without evidence. 'Going to' = plan already made or prediction based on present evidence: 'Look at those clouds — it's going to rain'.
'Shall' is formal/old-fashioned and mainly used with I/We in offers and suggestions: 'Shall I open the window?' In modern English 'will' is standard for all persons.

faq.q: Does 'will' always refer to the future?
faq.a: Not always. 'Will' can express a general truth or habit: 'Ice will melt at 0°C'. But in everyday speech it almost always means future.
